multiplechoiceresponse.label: You have 500 episodes but 40% have idle trajectories. What should you do?

choicegroup.choice: Train on all 500 — more data is always better
Curate first — remove idle episodes, train on the clean 300. Quality beats quantity.
Add more data to dilute the bad episodes
Increase the learning rate to compensate

solution.p: Bad data actively hurts training. 300 clean episodes will produce a better policy than 500 noisy ones. Always curate before training.

multiplechoiceresponse.label: Your policy achieves 95% success rate in simulation but only 30% on real hardware. What is the most likely cause?

choicegroup.choice: The model is too small
You need more training steps
Sim-to-real gap — the simulation doesn't match real-world physics, lighting, or sensor noise
The learning rate is too high

solution.p: A large gap between sim and real performance is the classic sim-to-real problem. Fix: add domain randomization to sim, collect some real data, add sensor noise to sim observations.

multiplechoiceresponse.label: What is the correct order of the Physical AI iteration loop?

choicegroup.choice: Train → Collect → Deploy → Evaluate
Design → Collect → Curate → Train → Evaluate → Identify failures → Collect targeted data → Retrain
Deploy → Collect → Train → Evaluate
Evaluate → Train → Collect → Deploy

solution.p: The loop always starts with design and collection, then training, then evaluation. Failures drive the next collection round. This iterative process is how Physical AI systems improve.

multiplechoiceresponse.label: A controller submits 100 episodes but they all start from the exact same object position. What problem does this create?

choicegroup.choice: The dataset is too small
Distribution shift — the policy will fail when the object is in any other position
The episodes are too short
No problem — consistency is good

solution.p: Coverage is essential. A policy trained on one object position can't generalize to other positions. You need variation in your dataset to cover the range of conditions the robot will encounter.

multiplechoiceresponse.label: Which model would you choose for a simple pick-and-place task with 80 clean episodes?

choicegroup.choice: ACT (Action Chunking Transformer) — designed for dexterous manipulation with small datasets
OpenVLA — needs 200+ episodes and is overkill for a simple task
Diffusion Policy — needs 100-500 episodes, more complex to train
SmolVLA — needs 500+ episodes for meaningful performance

solution.p: ACT is specifically designed for dexterous manipulation tasks and works well with 50-200 episodes. It's the right choice for a simple pick-and-place with a small dataset.

multiplechoiceresponse.label: What should a good job description for data collection include?

choicegroup.choice: Just the task name — controllers should figure out the details
The model architecture you plan to use
Task description, reset procedure, episode requirements, quality criteria, and what to avoid
The training hyperparameters

solution.p: A clear job description is the most important thing a trainer can provide. Controllers need to know exactly what to do, how to reset, how many episodes, and what quality looks like.

multiplechoiceresponse.label: Your policy consistently fails during the "grasp" phase. What is the best next step?

choicegroup.choice: Train for more steps
Switch to a larger model
Collect 50 episodes specifically focused on the grasp phase — approach, close gripper, verify grasp
Reduce the learning rate

solution.p: When a policy fails at a specific phase, the fix is targeted data collection for that phase. More training steps or a larger model won't help if the training data doesn't cover the failure mode.
